episode.title: Cariboo Signals - Community Tech & Governance
episode.description:
Riley and Casey explore technology and society in rural communities. Today's focus: Community Tech & Governance.

NEWS ROUNDUP: We break down [BC Gov News] Building volunteer fire department capacity and [Android Authority] Android Auto just made EV trip planning a lot smarter for these car brands, plus 1 more stories, and explore what these developments mean for communities like ours.

RURAL CONNECTIONS: How do information systems and governance structures in rural communities determine who has access to decision-making power, and whose interests do transparency measures actually serve? Topics include: Cougar alert systems and emergency communication infrastructure in rural BC, Canada Post mail delivery consolidation and loss of bundled community services, Aboriginal Title Transparency Act and strategic disclosure in Indigenous governance, Epistemic infrastructure and who controls information about community needs, Heiltsuk and other Indigenous-led information sovereignty models.

Hosts: Riley (Tech optimist with an engineering background) and Casey (Community-first skeptic of tech promises).

discussion.central_question: How do information systems and governance structures in rural communities determine who has access to decision-making power, and whose interests do transparency measures actually serve?
discussion.riley_position: Information infrastructure is critical but communities shouldn't wait for perfect systems—pragmatic volunteer-led solutions (like Facebook groups) work well enough in the interim, and the risk of over-waiting for ideal governance can leave people uninformed during crises.
riley_key_evidence: Facebook groups maintained by volunteers are currently the fastest and most reliable way to disseminate urgent safety information like cougar sightings in small communities
Labor law analogy showing mandatory disclosure of union organizing was framed as transparency but actually enabled employer counter-campaigns
The Heiltsuk Nation's post-Deepwater Horizon alert system demonstrates that community-controlled information infrastructure is buildable and effective
discussion.casey_position: Informal, volunteer-dependent information systems are fragile and ultimately reinforce information asymmetries that disadvantage rural communities; transparency measures can be designed to benefit powerful actors rather than empower communities, particularly Indigenous communities protecting negotiation positions.
casey_key_evidence: Canada Post's cost-per-route analysis doesn't account for bundled services like mail carriers performing informal wellness checks on isolated seniors
The Aboriginal Title Transparency Act could expose sensitive negotiating positions to resource extraction interests, turning transparency into a pressure tool against Indigenous rights
Rural municipalities lack the capacity to commission the social infrastructure research needed to advocate effectively against top-down federal decisions
discussion.resolution: They agreed that community-controlled information infrastructure is the goal and identified concrete middle-path solutions (SMS broadcast systems, municipal emergency contact databases, regional district-commissioned community mapping) rather than continuing to debate the perfect versus pragmatic tension.
